paragraphs.text: I would like to give two pieces of feedback. The first pertains to an issue that I do not yet see reflected in the initiative: the problem of video game publishers disabling so-called "live-service" games at whatever point they choose. Over the past two decades, more and more games operate on the live-service model, meaning that they cannot be played without a live internet connection that connects the player to the publisher's servers. That is not only true for online multi-player games but increasingly even for single-player games. Introducing the live-service model serves several purposes: Connecting different players with each other (in case of multi-player games); adding in-game stores and purchases to a game; collecting player behaviour data for analysis; anti-cheat measures; and more. There is nothing inherently wrong with this model. Without it, many popular multi-player games could not exist. The problem is that the game will only be playable as long as the publisher keeps hosting their central servers for players to connect to. As soon as the publisher decides to stop support for a game, it is rendered unplayable. Countless games have disappeared in this way over the past two decades, and recently, there was even a lawsuit filed against publisher Ubisoft for discontinuing the 2014 game The Crew. This practice is terrible not only for art preservation purposes (which is notoriously bad in the video game sphere) but also for consumer protection. As it stands, it is very easy for a consumer to buy a video game for full price and then watch as the game is rendered unplayable the next day, with no legal recourse. The game will be gone -- including any additional (e.g. aesthetic) items purchased in in-game stores. That's why the sum of money a player can lose in this way does not even stop at the game purchase price. Publishers justify this practice by arguing that the player never really owned the video game, he merely owned a licence that the publisher can invalidate at any time. That is indeed the current legislation. A Ubisoft spokesperson even publicly said that player should "get comfortable" with the idea of not owning their video games. And yet, across all video game stores they are asking you to "buy" and "purchase" their games, and they talk about "units sold". Never does it ask you to merely "rent" a game. That is consumer deception. I ask for publishers to be forced to devise end-of-life plans for their games. This does not (!) mean that they should support any one game until the end of time. It merely means that the game remains in a functionally playable state -- so that, for example, I am able to continue playing it alone at home without connecting to their central servers, or so that other players can host private servers that fulfill the same function. This is already being done with some retro online games, although most publishers will actively take down such player-run attempts to keep a game alive. Hence, I am asking for legislation that stops consumers from losing games (and in-game assets) they already paid for. My second piece of feedback pertains to the initiative's stance against gambling-like features in video games. I want to warn against overreach and not, for example, forbid all gambling-like features in video games. In my opinion, it is a valid way to monetize a game that is otherwise free to access for everyone. Like "real" gambling, it can in fact even be fun when done responsibly. I know that child gambling in games is a real problem, but I would hate to lose games or features as an adult due to other parents not supervising their children's use of their credit cards. Instead, please focus on the presentation (!) of gambling mechanics in-game: For example, always also showing the actual converted monetary price of an asset instead of only "crystals" or whatever the in-game currency is; and getting rid of saving bank/card details so that you cannot gamble with just a single button press.
